ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general raita entry; no specific mixed-vegetable raita code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific raita or vegetable-yogurt dish code. Plain yoghurt (A0CKP) used as closest parent; mixed vegetable raita is a prepared yogurt-based dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for raita or mixed vegetable yogurt side dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side-dish serving of mixed vegetable raita is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small bowl served alongside a main Indian meal such as biryani or curry.

nutrient_source.title: Raita (plain yogurt with vegetables) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for plain dahi (yogurt) and individual vegetables. Mixed vegetable raita values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain low-fat dahi (per 100 g: energy 60 kcal, protein 3.1 g, fat 1.5 g, carbohydrate 8.6 g) blended with typical mixed vegetable additions (cucumber, carrot, tomato, onion) at approximately 30% vegetable fraction. Final per-100g values represent a weighted composite consistent with standard raita formulations documented in Indian food composition literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of yogurt and yogurt-based foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mixed vegetable raita. GI of 36 is estimated from plain low-fat yogurt (GI ~36, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care 44(Suppl 1):S1-S232). The vegetable additions (cucumber, carrot, tomato) are low-GI foods that do not materially raise the overall GI. GL per serving = (36 × 7.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.9, rounded to one decimal place. GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.guidance: Mixed vegetable raita contains approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one small bowl (150 g) to keep sodium contribution below 270 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 36 and a glycemic load of ~3.9 per 150 g serving, mixed vegetable raita is a suitable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and can help moderate the overall glycemic response of a meal when served alongside higher-GI foods such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and beneficial dairy calcium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mixed vegetable raita made with low-fat yogurt provides approximately 0.9 g saturated fat per 100 g and supplies calcium and potassium; it is a heart-friendly side dish when prepared without cream or full-fat yogurt, and portion control is advised to manage overall caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Vegetable Raita (also spelled Raitha) is a traditional Indian yogurt-based side dish prepared by combining chilled plain yogurt (dahi) with finely chopped or grated raw vegetables such as cucumber, carrot, tomato, and onion, seasoned with roasted cumin powder, salt, and fresh coriander leaves. The dish has a cool, creamy, and mildly tangy flavour with a refreshing crunch from the vegetables. It is served cold and functions as a palate-cooling accompaniment to spicy Indian main dishes including biryani, pulao, curry, and tandoori preparations. Raita is a staple across North and South Indian cuisine and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, raita is a low-calorie, low-GI food providing approximately 55 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~2.8 g) from yogurt, low fat (~1.4 g), and useful amounts of calcium (~105 mg) and potassium (~185 mg). Its low glycemic index (~36) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content from added salt warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is naturally vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable raita?
What vegetables are in mixed veg raita?
Can I eat raita with biryani?
What is the difference between raita and tzatziki?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for mixed vegetable raita was found in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dahi (yogurt) values with a weighted adjustment for a typical 30% mixed vegetable fraction. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain low-fat yogurt, GI 36) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for dahi or the vegetable components used. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in plain low-fat yogurt per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate based on plain low-fat yogurt from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021). GL per 150 g serving = (36 × 7.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.9. This record is served by Anjana Kitchen - Six Battery Road on field survey data at SGD 9.00. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Mixed' to 'Dairy and eggs' as the primary ingredient is yogurt. Cuisine tags from input (Curry, Naan) are contextual serving associations rather than cuisine descriptors for raita itself.
